critique.summary: The article introduces ProofShot, an open-source tool designed to provide visual verification for AI coding agents by recording their UI interactions and generating proof artifacts. It allows developers to review AI agent actions through video recordings, error logs, and interactive timelines to ensure the correctness of the UI built by AI.
mainPoints: ProofShot records AI agent interactions with the UI including navigation, clicks, form fills, and screenshots.
It generates proof bundles containing video recordings, error logs, and screenshots for verification.
The tool provides an interactive HTML viewer to review sessions with synchronized video and action timelines.
It supports error detection by capturing browser console errors and scanning server logs for multiple programming languages.
ProofShot is agent-agnostic and works with various AI coding agents like Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and others.
The tool is open source under the MIT license and can be installed and set up quickly via CLI commands.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about ProofShot's functionality align with typical features of UI testing and verification tools, and the description is consistent with standard practices in software development for automated UI testing and logging. The tool being open source under the MIT license and supporting multiple AI agents is plausible and verifiable via its GitHub repository. There are no extraordinary or unsupported claims in the article, and the technical details provided match known methods for recording and verifying UI interactions.
considerations: The article does not mention any limitations or potential challenges in integrating ProofShot with complex or highly dynamic UIs.
It does not discuss the performance impact of running ProofShot alongside AI agents or the scalability of the tool for large projects.
There is no mention of privacy or security considerations when recording UI interactions, especially in sensitive environments.
The article does not provide user testimonials, case studies, or comparisons with similar tools to contextualize ProofShot's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on ProofShot, an AI tool that visually verifies UI changes by taking screenshots, contrasting it with tools like Playwright that check structural DOM properties. Commenters highlight its usefulness for catching visual errors that traditional testing misses, its potential in desktop and mobile app contexts, and comparisons to existing tools like agent-browser, Claude Code, and Antigravity.

comments.author: mrothroc
comments.text:
Everyone is comparing this to Playwright but it's solving a different problem. Playwright checks structural properties, like does element X exist, is it visible, etc. That's useful but it can't tell you whether the page actually looks right.

I built something similar that takes a screenshot and uses a multi-modal LLM to evaluate it against a design mock. It catches a completely different class of error. The DOM can be structurally perfect and still look nothing like what was intended. Colors wrong, layout shifted, spacing off, components overlapping. No amount of DOM assertions will catch that.

These are two different kinds of gates: structural which are fast and deterministic, and stochastic which are slow but catch things that are completely different. There is very little overlap between the issues, and you want to catch both.

That way I can invest a lot of time getting the mock just right, then let the agents "make it so".
comments.analysis: This comment accurately distinguishes between structural testing and visual validation, highlighting the complementary nature of ProofShot to existing tools.

comments.author: alkonaut
comments.text: This would be _extremely_ valuable for desktop dev when you don't have a DOM, no "accessibility" layer to interrogate. Think e.g. a drawing application. You want to test that after the user starts the "draw circle" command and clicks two points, there is actually a circle on the screen. No matter how many abstractions you make over your domain model, rendering you can't actually test that "the user sees a circle". You can verify your drawing contains a circle object. You can verify your renderer was told to draw a circle. But fifty things can go wrong before the user actually agrees he saw a circle (the color was set to transparent, the layer was hidden, the transform was incorrect, the renderer didn't swap buffers, ...).
comments.analysis: Provides a concrete use case for visual verification in desktop apps where traditional DOM-based testing is insufficient, reinforcing the tool's value.

comments.author: sd9
comments.text:
I've always found screenshots on PRs incredibly helpful as a reviewer. Historically I've had mixed success getting my team to consistently add screenshots to PRs, so this tool would be helpful even for human code.

At work, we've integrated claude code with gitlab issues/merge requests, and we get it to screenshot anything it's done. We could use the same workflow to screenshot (or in this case, host a proofshot bundle of) _any_ open PR. You would just get the agent to check out any PR, get proofshot to play around with it, then add that as a comment. So not automated code reviews, which are tiresome, but more like a helpful comment with more context.

Going to try out proofshot this week, if it works like it does on the landing page it looks great.
comments.analysis: Offers practical insights on integrating visual proofing into code review workflows, supporting the tool's usefulness beyond automated agents.

comments.author: jofzar
comments.text:
I'm going the opposite of everyone else is saying.

This is sick OP based on what's in the document, it looks really useful when you need to quickly fix something and need to validate the changes to make sure nothing has changed in the UI/workflow except what you have asked.

Also looks useful for PR's, have a before and after changed.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and practical utility of the tool for quick validation, though less detailed than other comments.

comments.author: varispeed
comments.text: I built something like this for native application, so that I could get automated feedback loop for the agent instead of making screenshots manually etc. Problem I found is that AI agent understands nothing of the UI. If you tell it "Make buttons evenly spaced", sure it will space them evenly, but without care for the context they are placed in. You have to describe the image yourself and still you'll find it having hard time understanding what's going on. I very much abandoned the idea of AI driven UI development as it is not there yet. I tried with GPT 5.2. Maybe newer models have improved.
comments.analysis: Provides a cautionary perspective based on experience, highlighting current limitations of AI in understanding UI context despite visual feedback.

comments.author: esafak
comments.text:
It looks similar to agent-video, which is also based on agent-browser: https://www.mux.com/blog/agentic-video-screen-recording

I don't think you need either, though, because agent-browser itself has a skill for this: https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser/blob/main/skill...

Maybe the author would like to compare the three.
comments.analysis: Offers useful references to related projects and suggests comparative evaluation, enhancing the discussion's depth.

comments.author: onion2k
comments.text:
I use AI agents to build UI features daily. The thing that kept annoying me: the agent writes code but never sees what it actually looks like in the browser. It can’t tell if the layout is broken or if the console is throwing errors.

I give agent either a simple browser or Playwright access to proper browsers to do this. It works quite well, to the point where I can ask Claude to debug GLSL shaders running in WebGL with it.
comments.analysis: Shares practical experience integrating visual feedback into AI agent workflows, supporting the need for tools like ProofShot.

comments.author: malloc2048
comments.text:
I usually ask Claude Code to setup a software stack that can build/run whatever I am working on. Then I let it browse a website or navigate through screens. I also use Playwright to get screenshots of the website I am building. For e.g. apps or whatever application you are building, there should be a way to get screenshots too I guess.

Added benefit is that when Claude navigates and finds a bug, it will either add them to a list for human review or fix it automatically.

Pretty much a loop where building and debugging work together;-)

Once Claude Code
comments.analysis: Describes an automated feedback loop combining AI navigation and screenshotting, illustrating practical applications of visual verification.

comments.author: alexovch
comments.text: This is actually interesting. Feels like we’re moving from “generate UI” to “validate UI,” which is a completely different problem. Curious how you handle edge cases where something looks correct but breaks in interaction?
comments.analysis: Raises a thoughtful question about the limits of visual validation, emphasizing the distinction between appearance and interaction correctness.

comments.author: _aravindc
comments.text: This is awesome, does it work with desktop application ?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about applicability beyond web, indicating user interest in broader use cases.
